summary: Jeff Atwood examines the recurring pattern of online communities self-destructing due to their inability to moderate themselves, drawing on Clay Shirky's influential essay and the cautionary tale of Kuro5hin's decline. The post traces this phenomenon back to 1978's Communitree BBS, which was overrun by high school students posting inappropriate content because its founders couldn't reconcile moderation with their open-access ideals. Atwood argues that technical and social problems are inseparable in community management, and that as communities grow, hybrid rules become essential for survival. The piece concludes with practical advice: developers building social software must plan for bad behavior from the start or face inevitable regret.
key_points: Online communities consistently follow a pattern where openness and democratic ideals eventually enable malicious users to drive away the original community
The distinction between 'technical' and 'social' problems in community software is false—they are deeply intertwined and inseparable
Any unmoderated group will eventually debate whether to add moderation, with the probability approaching certainty over time
Communities founded on principles of free speech and open access often lack the philosophical framework to defend themselves from abuse
Social software may not scale as traditionally conceived—growth creates structural demands that challenge founding ideals
money_quotes: Technical and social issues are deeply intertwined. There's no way to completely separate them.
The likelihood that any unmoderated group will eventually get into a flame-war about whether or not to have a moderator approaches one as time increases.
The place that was founded on open access had too much open access, too much openness. They couldn't defend themselves against their own users.
The place that was founded on free speech had too much freedom. They had no way of saying 'No, that's not the kind of free speech we meant.'
People are bastard coated bastards with bastard filling.
If you're building software with social components, plan for the worst kinds of behavior from your users from the start.
key_insight: Every online community will eventually need to develop hybrid technical-social controls to protect itself from its own users, and founders who fail to plan for this inevitability will watch their communities collapse.
